response:
I want to create something that puts people first. The numbers tell one story, but there are humans behind every churned account and every new hire.

Let me propose a Listening Architecture with three components. First, a Customer Voice Circle. Not a survey. Invite 15-20 recently churned customers to small, facilitated group conversations. Pay them for their time. Listen without defending. Ask: "What moment made you decide to leave?" The specificity matters more than statistics.

Second, an Engineer Empathy Rotation. Those 30 new engineers have never met a customer. Before they ship a single feature, pair each one with a customer success rep for two days. Let them hear support calls, read ticket threads, feel the friction. This builds product intuition no brief provides.

Third, a Team Health Check across existing engineering squads. Rapid scaling (30 new hires into 200 people is 15% growth) creates cultural dilution. The original team may be losing psychological safety, voice, or ownership. When builders feel disconnected from purpose, they ship features that technically work but emotionally miss.

The NPS score worries me precisely because it's high. It masks what happens at the edges. The 72 represents the center of your user base feeling generally positive. But churn happens at the margins, among users whose needs drifted from your roadmap. They don't complain; they leave.

Create spaces for those quiet signals to be heard.
